P.description: Adult patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes

I.description: receiving treatment intensification during a 120-day window following an index HbA1c measurement

C.description: patients who did not receive timely treatment intensification

secondary_outcomes: odds of achieving superior control (HbA1c level <8.0%) after treatment intensification

variables: number of unique pre-index oral antidiabetes agents
primary treating physician specialty (internist vs family medicine specialist)

parsed.sample_size: 547 patients met study selection criteria, with 480 patients used for statistical analyses

P.description: Consecutive patients with chronic liver disease (CLD) or cirrhosis
P.demographics: median age 53 years
P.setting: outpatient hepatology clinic in Ontario, Canada

variables: hierarchical algorithm incorporating laboratory and administrative codes
I.description: use of a hierarchical algorithm to define etiology of CLD and cirrhosis

C.description: gold standard liver disease etiology determined by an attending hepatologist

O.primary_outcome: diagnostic accuracy of the hierarchical algorithm
O.measurement: calculating sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and kappa’s agreement

parsed.causal_question: Does the hierarchical algorithm accurately define the etiology of chronic liver disease and cirrhosis in patients attending an outpatient hepatology clinic, compared to the gold standard clinical diagnosis?

P.description: Patients with diabetic macular oedema (DMO) treated with anti-VEGF therapy
P.demographics: adults, 1964 patients, male and female, no specific age range mentioned
P.setting: tertiary referral centre, Moorfields Eye Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, London, UK

variables: intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) injections
I.description: anti-VEGF therapy for DMO

C.description: no explicit comparison group, but implicitly comparing treated eyes to their own baseline or natural history

O.primary_outcome: visual acuity (VA) outcomes, specifically reaching a favourable visual outcome of more than 70 Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study letters
secondary_outcomes: time to fall below 70 letters after reaching it
O.measurement: Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) letters

parsed.causal_question: Does anti-VEGF therapy affect visual acuity outcomes in patients with diabetic macular oedema, compared to their own baseline or natural history, after adjusting for injection number, male gender, and baseline VA?

P.description: Patients undergoing below-the-knee amputation (BKA) due to various indications
P.demographics: age, sex (not explicitly reported but mentioned as a controlled variable), and other characteristics such as diabetes status and hemoglobin A1c level
P.setting: A Level 1 trauma center

I.description: Below-the-knee amputation surgery

C.description: No explicit comparison group, as this is an observational study focusing on outcomes after BKA

O.primary_outcome: Survivorship free from unplanned reoperation within 1 year of BKA index surgery
secondary_outcomes: Patient characteristics associated with reoperation within 1 year of BKA
O.measurement: Kaplan-Meier estimation and multivariate logistic regression analyses

parsed.causal_question: Does having a hemoglobin A1c level greater than 8.1% affect the odds of unplanned reoperation within 1 year after below-the-knee amputation in patients undergoing BKA, compared to those with an A1c level of 8.1% or less, after adjusting for potential confounders?

P.description: Women with low-risk pregnancies giving birth in hospitals
P.demographics: Full-term, uncomplicated pregnancies
P.setting: Hospital-based deliveries in two health systems (eight hospitals) in the United States

I.description: Water immersion during the first or second stage of labor

C.description: Women who met clinical eligibility criteria for waterbirth but did not experience water immersion during labor
C.type: Matched control group without water immersion

O.primary_outcome: Neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) or special care nursery admission
O.measurement: Electronic health record data and propensity score matching

parsed.sample_size: 583 women with water immersion and a matched control group
parsed.causal_question: Does water immersion during labor affect neonatal intensive care unit or special care nursery admission and other outcomes in women with low-risk pregnancies, compared to a matched control group without water immersion?

P.description: Patients visiting the emergency department (ED) of a large tertiary academic urban hospital
P.demographics: mean age 49.2 years, 59.4% female, 76.7% non-Hispanic
P.setting: Emergency department of a large tertiary academic urban hospital in Boston, Massachusetts

variables: Display of patient photographs in the electronic health record (EHR)
I.description: Noninterruptive display of patient photographs in the banner of the EHR

C.description: Orders placed without a patient photograph displayed in the EHR

O.primary_outcome: Rate of wrong-patient order entry (WPOE) errors
secondary_outcomes: Risk of WPOE errors in patients with higher acuity levels
Risk of WPOE errors among patients of different racial backgrounds
O.measurement: Retract-and-reorder method

parsed.causal_question: Does the display of patient photographs in the EHR affect the rate of wrong-patient order entry errors in patients visiting the emergency department, compared to orders placed without a patient photograph displayed, after adjusting for patient acuity level and race?
